perturbation.original_text: CONSULTANT'S REPRESENTATIONS & WARRANTIES. Consultant represents and warrants to the Company as follows: The Consultant (i) has adequate means of providing for the Consultant's current needs and possible personal contingencies; (ii) is acquiring the Shares for investment and not with a view to their distribution and has no need for liquidity in this investment; (iii) is able to bear the substantial economic risks of an investment in the Shares for an indefinite period; and (iv) is an "accredited investor" as defined in Rule 501 of Regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended.
perturbation.changed_text: NON-DISCLOSURE OF TERMS. Consultant represents and warrants to the Company as follows: The Consultant (i) has adequate means of providing for the Consultant's current needs and possible personal contingencies; (ii) is acquiring the Shares for investment and not with a view to their distribution and has no need for liquidity in this investment; (iii) is able to bear the substantial economic risks of an investment in the Shares for an indefinite period; and (iv) is an "accredited investor" as defined in Rule 501 of Regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'accredited investor' representation from 'Consultant's Representations & Warranties' to 'Non-Disclosure of Terms' obscures its relevance to securities law compliance. The accredited investor clause is a direct requirement under Regulation D for private placements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Regulation D of the Securities Act of 1933, Rule 506(b)(2)(ii)
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 506(b)(2)(ii) of Regulation D requires verification of accredited investor status, implying that this representation needs to be conspicuously placed within documents directly pertaining to securities offerings, not hidden under a confidentiality clause. Placing it under 'Non-Disclosure' weakens the emphasis on investor qualification, which is a key protection for unregistered securities offerings.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.506 - Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.506 Exemption for limited offers and sales without regard to dollar amount of offering. (a) Exemption. Offers and sales of securities by an issuer that satisfy the conditions in paragraph (b) or (c) of this section shall be deemed to be transactions not involving any public offering within the meaning of section 4(a)(2) of the Act . (b) Conditions to be met in offerings subject to limitation on manner of offering —(1) General conditions. To qualify for an exemption under this section, offers and sales must satisfy all the terms and conditions of §§ 230.501 and 230.502. (2) Specific conditions — (i) Limitation on number of purchasers. There are no more than, or the issuer reasonably believes that there are no more than, 35 purchasers of securities from the issuer in offerings under this section in any 90-calendar-day period. Note 1 to paragraph (b)(2)(i): See § 230.501(e) for the calculation of the number of purchasers and § 230.502(a) for what may or may not constitute an offering under paragraph (b) of this section. (ii) Nature of purchasers. Each purchaser who is not an accredited investor either alone or with his purchaser representative(s) has such knowledge and experience in financial and business matters that he is capable of evaluating the merits and risks of the prospective investment, or the issuer reasonably believes immediately prior to making any sale that such purchaser comes within this description. perturbation.justification: The modified contract moves the 'accredited investor' representation from a general section on 'Consultant's Representations & Warranties' to a section on 'Non-Disclosure of Terms'. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation argue this obscures its relevance to securities law compliance under Regulation D. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 17 CFR § 230.506, discusses exemptions for limited offers and sales, and paragraph (b)(2)(ii) mentions the 'nature of purchasers,' requiring that non-accredited investors possess sufficient financial knowledge. By placing the accredited investor representation under 'Non-Disclosure,' the changed text weakens the emphasis on investor qualification, which is a key protection for unregistered securities offerings, creating a structural flaw. This weakens the enforceability of the accredited investor requirement under securities laws, thereby creating a legal contradiction as per the structural flaws definition.

perturbation.original_text: SEC & LEGAL COMPLIANCE. Consultant hereby represents that it has in place policies and procedures relating to, and addressing, with the commercially reasonable intent to ensure compliance with, all applicable securities laws, rules and regulations, including but not limited to the use, release or other publication of forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act and Section 21E of the Exchange Act and disclosure requirements outlined in Section 17B of the Exchange Act regarding the required disclosure of the nature and terms of its relationship with the Company in any and all literature or other communication(s) relating to these services, including but not limited to: Press Releases, letters to investors and telephone or other personal communication(s) with potential or current investors.
perturbation.changed_text: TERM & TERMINATION. Consultant hereby represents that it has in place policies and procedures relating to, and addressing, with the commercially reasonable intent to ensure compliance with, all applicable securities laws, rules and regulations, including but not limited to the use, release or other publication of forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act and Section 21E of the Exchange Act and disclosure requirements outlined in Section 17B of the Exchange Act regarding the required disclosure of the nature and terms of its relationship with the Company in any and all literature or other communication(s) relating to these services, including but not limited to: Press Releases, letters to investors and telephone or other personal communication(s) with potential or current investors.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Consultant's SEC compliance representation to 'Term & Termination' significantly diminishes its prominence. While present, it's no longer readily apparent in a section concerning legal oversight, weakening its importance for due diligence and compliance checks. This relocation misplaces a crucial disclosure about adhering to securities laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 17(b) of the Securities Act of 1933
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 17(b) mandates the disclosure of consideration received for promoting securities. By placing the compliance representation under 'Term & Termination,' the direct link between the consultant's adherence to disclosure requirements and the actual receipt of compensation for promotional activities is obscured, making it harder to enforce the consultant's disclosure obligations related to payments received for promotional activity as required by the Act.

perturbation.justification: The relocation of the SEC compliance representation from a dedicated 'SEC & LEGAL COMPLIANCE' section to 'TERM & TERMINATION' undermines the prominence and enforceability of the consultant's adherence to securities laws. The law explanation states that Section 17(b) mandates disclosure of consideration received for promoting securities, and by placing the compliance representation under 'Term & Termination,' the direct link between adherence to disclosure requirements and compensation for promotional activities is obscured. This creates a structural flaw, as defined in the prompt. While the clause itself is still present, its placement significantly weakens its legal effect regarding compliance with disclosure obligations under Section 17(b), potentially making it harder to enforce, thus contradicting the intent of the law. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 15 U.S.C. 77q, which concerns fraudulent interstate transactions and is directly relevant to securities law compliance. Placing the compliance clause in an obscure place contradicts the aim of this statute.
